12.1.title: Arjuna's chapter-opening fork
12.1.body: Two stances are juxtaposed via ये ... ये च — "those who [worship the personal you]" vs "those who [worship the unmanifest]." The question तेषां के योगवित्तमाः = "of those, who are the best yoga-knowers?" the षष्ठी तेषाम् governs the comparative-superlative योग-वित्-तम (वित् = "knower" + तम = "-est" superlative).

12.1.title: Two objects of the same verb
12.1.body: पर्युपासते ("closely worship") governs two parallel द्वितीयाs — त्वाम् ("you," Krishna personal) and अक्षरम् अव्यक्तम् ("the imperishable, the unmanifest"). Same verb, two devotional objects. The whole chapter's pedagogy hangs on which gets ranked above the other.

12.2.title: Krishna's answer comes in absolutives
12.2.body: मयि आवेश्य मनः ... नित्य-युक्ताः उपासते = "having entered the mind into me, [they] worship as ever-yoked." The absolutive आवेश्य stages the prerequisite; the finite verb उपासते is the action. Grammar carries the pedagogy: locate the mind in me FIRST, then worship.

12.2.title: युक्ततमा — superlative "most yoked"
12.2.body: ते मे युक्ततमा मताः = "they are considered most-yoked by me." तृतीया मे ("by me") + nominative प्रेडिcate युक्ततमा + PPP मताः ("deemed"). Krishna ranks: the personal-worshipper, मेरे-mind-entered, श्रद्धा-equipped, is the highest yogi.

12.3.title: Six attributes of the unmanifest, all द्वितीया
12.3.body: ये अक्षरम् अनिर्देश्यम् अव्यक्तं पर्युपासते सर्वत्रगम् अचिन्त्यं च कूटस्थम् अचलं ध्रुवम् — eight adjectives, all in the same द्वितीया एकवचन agreeing with the implicit object "that-which-they-worship." Sanskrit's case-agreement makes the list legible at a glance: every modifier is the SAME case, so they all modify the same thing.

12.3.title: Negative-bahuvrīhi cascade
12.3.body: अनिर्देश्यम् ("not-pointable-to") + अव्यक्तम् ("un-manifest") + अचिन्त्यम् ("un-thinkable") + अचलम् ("un-moving"). The negation-prefix अ- is doing morphological heavy lifting — each modifier names the unmanifest by what it ISN'T. Six attributes via four negations + two positives (सर्वत्रग, ध्रुव).

12.4.title: Two absolutives + present participle
12.4.body: संनियम्य इन्द्रिय-ग्रामम् ("having restrained the collection-of-senses") + सर्वत्र समबुद्धयः ("equal-minded everywhere") + सर्व-भूत-हिते रताः ("delighting in the welfare of all beings"). The third is a present participle (रत = "taking-delight") in समबुद्धयः's apposition. Three modifiers — restraint, equanimity, universal welfare — stack before the verse delivers its verdict.

12.4.title: ते प्राप्नुवन्ति मामेव — they also reach me
12.4.body: Krishna's diplomatic concession: यes, the unmanifest-worshippers ALSO reach "me" (माम्). The एव is critical — "they reach me (not some lesser destination)." But 12.5 will name the cost. The verse is the carrot before the stick.

12.5.title: Comparative + ablative — "greater than"
12.5.body: क्लेशः अधिकतरः तेषाम् ... = "the difficulty (klesha) is greater for them." अधिकतरः is the comparative; तेषाम् (षष्ठी) marks "for whom." The unmanifest-path is harder, full stop. Krishna doesn't disparage it; he just names the cost.

12.5.title: हि — the metaphysical because
12.5.body: अव्यक्ता हि गतिः दुःखं देहवद्भिः अवाप्यते = "for the unmanifest goal is attained with-difficulty by the embodied." तृतीया देहवद्भिः ("by the embodied," मतुप् compound) is instrumental-of-agent in the passive. The reason: bodies are oriented toward the manifest; the unmanifest is upstream of their grain.

12.6.title: Triple modifier of the bhakta
12.6.body: मयि संन्यस्य ("having renounced [actions] into me") + मत्-पराः ("who hold me as supreme," बहुव्रीहि) + अनन्येन एव योगेन ("with non-other-yoga"). Three modifiers stack on the subject ये ("those who"). The 12.6-7 pair is the chapter's tightest statement of what bhakti looks like.

12.6.title: Two absolutives, one verb
12.6.body: संन्यस्य and ध्यायन्तः (present participle: "meditating") both prepare for the finite verb उपासते. The sequence: surrender actions → meditate without other-thoughts → THEN worship. Sanskrit packs the prerequisite chain into participial-absolutive grammar.

12.7.title: Future-तेन-नचिरात् promise
12.7.body: तेषाम् अहम् समुद्धर्ता ... भवामि = "I become their rescuer." तेषाम् is षष्ठी; समुद्धर्ता is agentive noun ("raiser-up"). न-चिरात् ("not-from-long" = soon) makes the rescue prompt. लट् भवामि ("I become") expresses what happens in the relational present.

12.7.title: मृत्यु-संसार-सागरात् — the ablative of rescue
12.7.body: Three-stem chain compound: "the ocean of the saṃsāra of death." पञ्चमी governs समुद्धर्ता ("raiser FROM"). The image is precise: not just from samsāra, but from the OCEAN of samsāra of death. Each layer of compound is a layer of imprisonment.

12.8.title: Three imperatives, one promise
12.8.body: मनः आधत्स्व ("place mind") + बुद्धिं निवेशय ("settle intellect") → निवसिष्यसि ("you will dwell") + न संशयः ("no doubt"). Three लोट् 2sg imperatives followed by a लृट् 2sg future. The grammar IS the practice: two acts of placing, one future certainty.

12.8.title: Repeated locative मयि — the housing
12.8.body: मयि एव ... मयि ... मयि एव — three repetitions of the same locative pronoun. Krishna's pedagogy is mantric: just locate yourself THERE. The एव (twice) seals "only there, not elsewhere."

12.9.title: First fallback: अभ्यास-योग
12.9.body: अथ चित्तं समाधातुं न शक्नोषि = "now if you cannot fix mind steadily on me" — Krishna acknowledges difficulty. Then offers fallback: अभ्यास-योगेन ततः माम् इच्छ आप्तुम् = "by the yoga of practice, then desire to attain me." तृतीया अभ्यास-योगेन is instrumental of means; इच्छ + आप्तुम् is imperative + infinitive.

12.9.title: धनञ्जय — vocative bookend
12.9.body: Krishna addresses Arjuna by his wealth-conqueror epithet at the START of the cascade of fallbacks. The choice is rhetorical: even YOU, the conqueror-of-wealth, may not manage the direct path — here are easier rungs.

12.10.title: Second fallback: मत्-कर्म-परम भव
12.10.body: अभ्यासे अपि असमर्थः असि = "if you can't do even practice" — Krishna keeps lowering the bar. Then: मत्-कर्म-परमः भव = "be one whose supreme aim is my work." The बहुव्रीहि (मत्-कर्म-परम = "my-work as supreme") describes the practitioner; भव is लोट् 2sg of √भू.

12.10.title: मत्-अर्थम् कर्माणि — adverbial -अर्थम्
12.10.body: मद्-अर्थम् अपि कर्माणि कुर्वन् = "even doing actions for my sake." The कृत्यर्थ -अर्थम् adverb governs the actions. Present participle कुर्वन् describes the agent. Sanskrit packages "by doing for my sake" into participle + adverbial कृत्यर्थ — one compact grammatical unit.

12.11.title: Third fallback: drop the fruit
12.11.body: अथ एतद् अपि अशक्तः असि कर्तुम् = "now if you are unable to do even this." Then: सर्व-कर्म-फल-त्यागम् ततः कुरु = "do the renunciation of all-action-fruit." Four-stem compound सर्व-कर्म-फल-त्याग ("renunciation of the fruit of all action") as द्वितीया object of कुरु.

12.11.title: मद्-योग-आश्रित — taking refuge in my yoga
12.11.body: मद्-योगम् आश्रितः = "having taken refuge in my yoga" (PPP, द्वितीया governing आश्रित). The bhakti-yoga is the SHELTER; renunciation of fruits is what you DO once sheltered. यतात्मवान् ("self-restrained," मतुप्) describes the resulting state.

12.12.title: The five-stage ladder of preference
12.12.body: Krishna ranks: अभ्यास < ज्ञान < ध्यान < कर्म-फल-त्याग < शान्ति. Four comparatives with पञ्चमी: "better-than-X." अभ्यासात् ("than practice") + ज्ञानात् ("than knowledge") + ध्यानात् ("than meditation") + त्यागात् ("than renunciation"). The instrumental अनन्तरम् ("immediately following") closes: "after renunciation, peace."

12.12.title: Why surprising — knowledge < renunciation
12.12.body: Krishna ranks ज्ञान BELOW ध्यान BELOW कर्म-फल-त्याग. Unusual for a Sanskrit text — usually knowledge is the apex. The Gītā's path: knowledge is necessary but not sufficient; only the ACTUAL renunciation of fruits brings peace. The grammatical ladder argues against intellectualism.

12.13.title: Six attributes of the dear bhakta — series 1
12.13.body: अद्वेष्टा ("non-hater," agentive of -द्वेष् + nañ), मैत्रः ("friendly"), करुणः ("compassionate"), निर्ममः ("non-mine-claiming," बहुव्रीहि नञ्), निरहङ्कारः ("non-ego," बहुव्रीहि नञ्), समदुःखसुखः ("equal-pain-pleasure," बहुव्रीहि), क्षमी ("forgiving," मतुप्). Krishna lists what makes the bhakta dear via six nominative singular adjectives — sequential predicates all describing the same implicit subject.

12.13.title: बहुव्रीहि compounds carry the negation
12.13.body: Three of the six modifiers use बहुव्रीहि-नञ् (compound + negative prefix): निर्-मम, निर्-अहङ्कार. "One whose mine-ness is gone" / "one whose ego is gone." The negation is built into the morphology — Sanskrit can't say "non-X" as a free adjective; it has to construct the whole bahuvrīhi. The grammar IS the spiritual psychology.

12.14.title: Four more attributes — the dedication string
12.14.body: सन्तुष्टः ("contented"), सततं योगी ("ever-yogi"), यतात्मा ("self-restrained," बहुव्रीहि), दृढ-निश्चयः ("firm-resolved," बहुव्रीहि). Four nominatives in apposition with the implicit सः. Then the chapter's refrain: सः मे प्रियः ("he is dear to me").

12.14.title: अर्पित-मनो-बुद्धि — the offering
12.14.body: मयि अर्पित-मनः-बुद्धिः = "one whose mind-and-intellect have been offered to me." बहुव्रीहि with locative मयि. Three-stem compound (अर्पित + मनस् + बुद्धि) packed as "having-given-up"-construct. Sanskrit's compound-richness makes this 5-word English phrase one Sanskrit modifier.

12.15.title: Two-direction noise-immunity
12.15.body: यस्मात् लोकः न उद्विजते = "from whom the world is not agitated" (पञ्चमी ablative — "caused-by-whom") + लोकात् यः न उद्विजते = "who is not agitated by the world" (पञ्चमी again). Same verb उद्विजते, opposite directionality. The bhakta neither rocks the boat nor gets rocked.

12.15.title: Four-element द्वंद्व chain in तृतीया
12.15.body: हर्ष-अमर्ष-भय-उद्वेगैः मुक्तः = "free from joy, intolerance, fear, agitation" (तृतीया-of-separation: "freed-from"). Four-element द्वंद्व compound + तृतीया + PPP मुक्तः. Sanskrit packs the emotional shopping-list into one compound noun in instrumental case.

12.16.title: Six bahuvrīhi adjectives in apposition
12.16.body: अनपेक्षः ("without-expectation"), शुचिः ("pure"), दक्षः ("competent"), उदासीनः ("indifferent"), गत-व्यथः ("whose pain has gone," बहुव्रीहि), सर्व-आरम्भ-परित्यागी ("renouncer of all undertakings"). Six modifiers, six nominative singular masculine, all describing the same one — the implicit "he."

12.16.title: उदासीन — the diplomatic posture
12.16.body: "Sitting apart / indifferent." From उद् + आस् ("sit up / sit removed"). Krishna doesn't say the bhakta is RUDE or COLD — उदासीन is a specific posture: present but not partisan, available but not invested. The Sanskrit captures what English calls "detached" without the chill.

12.17.title: Four-fold negation defining the bhakta
12.17.body: यः न हृष्यति न द्वेष्टि न शोचति न काङ्क्षति = "who does not exult, does not hate, does not grieve, does not crave." Four parallel न + लट् 3sg verbs. Sanskrit's negation + present indicative is the simplest framework for ongoing dispositional absence.

12.17.title: शुभ-अशुभ-परित्यागी — d̥वंद्व + agentive
12.17.body: "Renouncer of the good and the evil." शुभ-अशुभ is a छ्व्म्ब् इतरेतर द्वंद्व; -त्यागी is agentive ("-er suffix"). The bhakta has transcended the moral binary — not by being evil, but by being unhooked from BOTH poles.

12.18.title: Three locative द्वंद्वs of equanimity
12.18.body: समः शत्रौ च मित्रे च (locative dual implied) + मान-अपमानयोः (षष्ठी/सप्तमी dual) + शीत-उष्ण-सुख-दुःखेषु (locative plural). Three different cases ALL marking the contexts in which sameness obtains. Sanskrit's locative-of-domain: equanimity IN these specific arenas.

12.18.title: सङ्ग-विवर्जित — the diagnostic of equanimity
12.18.body: PPP तृतीया तत्पुरुष: "forsaken by attachment." One word for what makes the sameness possible. Krishna isn't asking for muscular suppression of feeling — he's naming the absence of clinging as the root of the equanimity.

12.19.title: Four-fold portrait of the saint
12.19.body: तुल्य-निन्दा-स्तुतिः ("to whom blame and praise are alike," बहुव्रीहि) + मौनी ("silent / introspective," इन्-stem) + सन्तुष्टः येन केनचित् ("satisfied by whatever") + अनिकेतः ("homeless," बहुव्रीहि-नञ्) + स्थिर-मतिः ("firm-minded," बहुव्रीहि). Five modifiers, three of which are compounds. The bhakta is grammatically constructed out of compound-adjectives.

12.19.title: येन केनचित् — the universal acceptor
12.19.body: तृतीया केनचित् = "by anything-whatsoever." चित् is the indefinitiser. Sanskrit packs "whatever comes" into a single instrumental-indefinite. The bhakta is whatever-content.

12.20.title: Chapter-closing superlative
12.20.body: ते अतीव मे प्रियाः = "they are exceedingly dear to me." अतीव intensifies the entire chapter's refrain. The verse names the supreme bhakta: श्रद्दधानाः ("having śraddhā," मतुप् participle) + मत्-परमाः ("holding me as supreme," बहुव्रीहि) + भक्ताः ("devotees").

12.20.title: धर्म्य-अमृत idam — the teaching as nectar
12.20.body: धर्म्यम् अमृतम् इदम् यथा-उक्तम् = "this righteous nectar, as it has been spoken." The whole BG 12 chapter is named अमृत. यथा-उक्तम् is PPP "as-stated" — Krishna closes by self-quoting. The chapter's teaching itself becomes the object of पर्युपासते ("closely worship").
